production_security_status: PARTIAL — the admin console's client-side authentication (formerly the sole CRITICAL blocker) is resolved as of PR #65: admin login is now server-verified with a signed httpOnly session. Production security as a whole is still NOT fully complete — see known_limitations below before treating this system as production-ready.

known_limitations.description: Content-Security-Policy (lib/security/security-headers.ts) uses script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' rather than a nonce-based policy. A nonce+'strict-dynamic' CSP was built first and reverted after real headless-browser testing showed it breaks every statically-prerendered page in this app (most routes) — a per-request nonce can never match script tags baked into HTML built once at compile time. The current CSP still blocks loading scripts from external/attacker-controlled origins, but cannot block inline-script injection the way a nonce-based policy would. Full protection would require moving affected pages to dynamic rendering — an architecture/performance change, not undertaken here.

distributed_rate_limiting closed this gap for login/admin-login (both Node.js runtime routes), but POST /api/webhooks runs on the Edge runtime (export const runtime = 'edge' in apps/web/src/app/api/webhooks/route.ts) and its rate limit + replay guard (lib/security/rate-limit.ts, lib/security/replay-guard.ts) remain in-memory/per-instance. ioredis (a raw-TCP client) needs Node's net/tls modules, which the Edge runtime doesn't have — attempting to share it there is not a matter of more code, it's a real architectural fork.
known_limitations.decision_needed: Two real options, not yet chosen: (A) adopt an Edge-compatible REST-based Redis client (e.g. Upstash Redis / Vercel KV) for the webhook route specifically — a concrete vendor choice with its own account/provisioning; (B) move POST /api/webhooks off the Edge runtime to the Node.js runtime so the same ioredis-backed limiter used for login/admin-login can cover it too — an architecture change to a route explicitly built as 'Module 13: Edge Webhook Byte Handling'. Neither was chosen unilaterally; flagged for Product Owner decision rather than silently picked.

known_limitations.description: PR #65 made admin login server-verified (signed httpOnly/Secure/SameSite=Strict session, real POST /api/admin/session endpoint) but deliberately kept the single shared ADMIN_ACCESS_TOKEN 'operator secret' model rather than moving to real per-admin user accounts. Every operator who knows the one secret is indistinguishable from any other in logs/audit trail, and there is no per-person revocation — only rotating the single shared secret revokes everyone at once. Real per-admin accounts (tied to the User table, role-based, individually attributable) were evaluated and deferred as a separate, larger, future milestone — deliberately out of scope for PR #65 to avoid touching the regular user auth system.

lib/search/embedding-provider.ts requires EMBEDDING_API_BASE_URL + EMBEDDING_API_KEY to use a real semantic embedding model (any HTTP endpoint matching the OpenAI embeddings API shape). Without them, a deterministic local 'feature hashing' provider is used so indexing/hybrid search stay fully functional in dev/CI, but vector similarity is only as good as shared-vocabulary overlap, not real semantic understanding. This degrades gracefully rather than failing (unlike S3/Redis, which 503/hard-fail when unconfigured), so it is not enforced by env-validation.ts — but production deployments should set both vars for real search quality.

database_foundation.description: Replaced in-memory DatabaseClient simulation with a real PostgreSQL connection (raw pg driver, no ORM). db/schema.sql applied via scripts/db/migrate.js. Tenant RLS verified against a real database with FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY.
database_foundation.provider: Supabase PostgreSQL (pending project provisioning — see PR)

real_authentication_and_user_storage.description: POST /api/auth/session validates bcrypt-hashed credentials against a real User row and mints a signed httpOnly JWT session cookie. /dashboard/* is now actually enforced by middleware (previously matched in the config but not actually gated — a real pre-existing bug, not a regression). Logout invalidates the session. User.email is now globally unique and User.password_hash was added. Verified against real PostgreSQL, bcryptjs, and a running server end-to-end.
real_authentication_and_user_storage.not_yet_true: Authentication is real; tenant AUTHORIZATION is not. A valid session proves who the user is, not that their tenant matches what they are accessing — that is the next milestone, not this one. General LegalCase/Tenant domain CRUD was explicitly out of scope and was not touched.

server_enforced_tenant_authorization.description: POST /api/documents/upload now derives tenant identity exclusively from the verified session cookie (lib/auth/session.ts requireSession()); client-supplied x-nextcase-tenant-id/x-tenant-id headers are ignored entirely, closing a real cross-tenant spoofing gap the route previously had. Verified end-to-end against a running server: an authenticated Tenant A request with forged headers claiming Tenant B still resolves to Tenant A.
server_enforced_tenant_authorization.scope_note: Deliberately limited to this one existing route per Product Owner decision. /api/webhooks was not modified. No general domain CRUD or advocate-facing features were added.
server_enforced_tenant_authorization.not_yet_true: Only /api/documents/upload has server-enforced tenant authorization. Other routes (e.g. /api/webhooks, the disconnected /api/* Bearer-token gate) are unchanged. There is still no real domain CRUD (LegalCase/DocumentEnvelope) to authorize against beyond this one mock ingestion endpoint.

secure_webhook_verification.description: POST /api/webhooks now requires an HMAC-SHA256 signature (x-nextcase-signature) over `${timestamp}.${rawBody}` plus a fresh x-nextcase-timestamp (5-minute tolerance, lib/security/webhook-signature.ts). Unsigned, invalid, tampered, expired, and replayed requests are rejected before the body is ever parsed as JSON. Replay protection (lib/security/replay-guard.ts) is an in-memory guard scoped to the tolerance window.
secure_webhook_verification.scope_note: Deliberately limited to this one existing route. No general domain CRUD or advocate-facing features were added; the payload processing behavior is otherwise unchanged (still a mock, no real persistence).
secure_webhook_verification.not_yet_true: Replay protection is per-process, in-memory state — correct for a single instance, but a horizontally-scaled multi-instance Edge deployment needs a shared store (Redis or a DB table) for replay protection to hold across instances. This is flagged as known follow-up infrastructure work, not silently assumed away.

production_security_hardening.status_note: The six approved items for this milestone are complete and merged (PR #63, commit 05b4588). This status covers ONLY that scoped work — it is NOT a claim that production security overall is complete. See top-level critical_production_blockers and known_limitations.
production_security_hardening.description: Security headers (CSP/HSTS/X-Frame-Options/X-Content-Type-Options/Referrer-Policy/Permissions-Policy) applied globally via next.config.ts headers(). In-memory rate limiting (lib/security/rate-limit.ts) on login (10/5min) and webhooks (60/min) per client IP. Origin/Referer CSRF checks (lib/security/origin-check.ts) on login, logout, and upload. Tenant-context cookie now sets Secure over HTTPS. instrumentation.ts + lib/security/env-validation.ts refuse to boot in NODE_ENV=production if JWT_SECRET, WEBHOOK_SIGNING_SECRET, or ADMIN_ACCESS_TOKEN are missing or equal their known-insecure placeholder values. The admin gate's token moved from a hardcoded literal in middleware.ts to ADMIN_ACCESS_TOKEN, compared in constant time.
production_security_hardening.design_correction: CSP was first implemented with a per-request nonce + 'strict-dynamic' (Next.js's documented pattern), then reverted after real headless-browser testing showed it breaks every statically-prerendered page (the majority of routes in this app): a nonce generated per-request can never match anything baked into HTML built once at compile time, so every script gets blocked. Replaced with a static script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' CSP — see known_limitations.static_csp_no_inline_script_protection for what this does and does not protect against. Verified with zero console/CSP errors and a real UI-driven login flow (form fill -> submit -> fetch -> cookie -> client-side navigation) via headless Chromium.
production_security_hardening.scope_note: No UI changes, no performance work, no database redesign, no Redis/distributed replay protection, no new features, per Product Owner scope.
production_security_hardening.not_yet_true: See known_limitations (static_csp_no_inline_script_protection, in_memory_rate_limiting_and_replay_protection) for what this milestone explicitly does not resolve. The admin_panel_client_side_login blocker this milestone left open was resolved separately by server_verified_admin_authentication below.

server_verified_admin_authentication.description: Replaced the admin console's entirely client-side login (hardcoded bypass strings "admin"/"nchq-admin-ops-2026" in shipped JS, writing a non-httpOnly cookie whose exact value was also hardcoded) with a real server-verified flow. POST /api/admin/session verifies the submitted key against ADMIN_ACCESS_TOKEN (constant-time, rate-limited, origin-checked) and mints a signed, httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict session cookie (lib/security/admin-session.ts, its own ADMIN_SESSION_SECRET). GET checks an existing session; POST /api/admin/logout clears it server-side. middleware.ts's /api/admin/* gate now verifies that signed JWT instead of comparing a raw shared secret against a client-writable cookie.
server_verified_admin_authentication.scope_decision: Kept the existing single shared 'operator secret' model (server-verified) rather than moving to real per-admin user accounts tied to the User table — the smaller, isolated change that doesn't touch the regular user auth system (lib/auth/session.ts, jwt.ts) or require a schema change, per Product Owner instruction not to redesign the admin UI or modify unrelated authentication flows. See known_limitations.admin_auth_shared_secret_no_individual_accountability for the tradeoff this leaves open.
server_verified_admin_authentication.scope_note: apps/web/src/app/admin/page.tsx markup, labels, tabs, and mock console data are byte-for-byte unchanged — only the login/logout/session-check handlers were rewired. No changes to /api/auth/*, lib/auth/session.ts, lib/auth/jwt.ts, or the User table.
server_verified_admin_authentication.not_yet_true: See known_limitations.admin_auth_shared_secret_no_individual_accountability. Everything else recorded under production_security_status/known_limitations (static CSP, in-memory rate-limit/replay) is unchanged by this milestone.

distributed_rate_limiting.status_note: Closes the in-memory-rate-limiting gap for POST /api/auth/session and POST /api/admin/session, the two routes where a standard TCP Redis client can run. Does NOT cover POST /api/webhooks (Edge runtime) — see known_limitations.webhook_rate_limit_and_replay_still_in_memory_edge_runtime for why, and the two real options awaiting a Product Owner decision.
distributed_rate_limiting.description: lib/security/redis-client.ts (lazy ioredis singleton, returns null when REDIS_URL is unset) and lib/security/redis-rate-limit.ts (checkDistributedRateLimit: Redis INCR+PEXPIRE fixed-window counter, same semantics as the existing in-memory limiter, falls back to it automatically when REDIS_URL is unset or Redis is unreachable). POST /api/auth/session and POST /api/admin/session now call checkDistributedRateLimit instead of the in-memory checkRateLimit. env-validation.ts now refuses to boot NODE_ENV=production without REDIS_URL set. CI's test job gained a real redis:7 service container so the Redis-backed code path is exercised in CI, not just the fallback.
distributed_rate_limiting.verification: Verified against a real local Redis instance (not mocked): unit tests prove atomic counting and a shared-across-instances property; runtime verification started two fully independent `next start` OS processes on different ports pointed at the same Redis and confirmed the second process immediately inherited the first process's exhausted rate-limit state for the same client IP — the actual property this milestone exists to provide, not just unit-tested in isolation. Also verified the REDIS_URL-unset fallback path still passes the full test suite unchanged.
distributed_rate_limiting.scope_note: Did not touch /api/webhooks, replay-guard.ts, or rate-limit.ts (both remain exactly as they were, still used by the webhook route). No UI changes, no new features.

real_case_management_api.status_note: The first real, RLS-backed domain CRUD API. Backend only by design: the existing /cases, /cases/[id], /cases/new, /matters pages still render mock/local-state data and were NOT touched — wiring them to this real API is its own follow-up milestone, not bundled in here.
real_case_management_api.description: POST/GET /api/cases and GET/PATCH/DELETE /api/cases/[id] persist real rows in the existing LegalCase table (db/schema.sql — schema and RLS policies unchanged, already present since the database foundation milestone). Tenant identity comes exclusively from the verified session cookie (lib/auth/session.ts requireSession()), never a client-supplied field; every query runs through DatabaseClient.execute(tenantId, ...) so Postgres RLS enforces isolation independent of application code. A request for a real case ID belonging to another tenant returns 404 (matching actual RLS behavior — zero rows, not a permission error), on GET, PATCH, and DELETE alike. middleware.ts's disconnected Bearer-token gate now exempts /api/cases, same pattern as /api/documents/upload.
real_case_management_api.verification: Verified against a real running server (not just unit tests): full create -> list -> get -> patch -> delete lifecycle over real HTTP with real session cookies for two separate tenants, confirming list scoping, cross-tenant 404 isolation on GET/PATCH, and correct write/delete behavior end-to-end — not simulated within a single test process.
real_case_management_api.scope_note: No UI changes. No changes to DocumentEnvelope, search, AI, billing, or messaging. No changes to the schema — LegalCase and its RLS policies already existed.
real_case_management_api.not_yet_true: The UI still shows mock data on every case-related page; this milestone only proves the real backend exists and is tenant-safe. DocumentEnvelope (real file/document persistence) is a separate, not-yet-started milestone.

case_management_api_hardening.status_note: Closes the gap between real_case_management_api's initial CRUD and production-readiness, per explicit Product Owner priority to finish the backend fully before starting UI work.
case_management_api_hardening.description: All /api/cases and /api/cases/[id] handlers now wrap their logic in try/catch, returning a consistent {error:'INTERNAL_ERROR'} 500 JSON response on unexpected failures (e.g. a DB outage) instead of falling through to Next.js's default error page — matching the established convention already used by /api/webhooks, /api/documents/upload, and /api/admin/session. GET /api/cases now accepts ?limit= (default 50, max 100) and ?offset= (default 0) query params, validated via zod, and returns {cases, total, limit, offset} — total is a real tenant-scoped COUNT(*), so callers can page through more than 100 cases, which the prior hardcoded LIMIT 100 with no way to see more or know the true count could not do.
case_management_api_hardening.verification: Verified against a real running server and real Postgres: created 5 cases for one tenant, fetched limit=2/offset=0 and limit=2/offset=2 pages and confirmed no ID overlap between them plus a correct total=5 on both pages; confirmed out-of-range limit and negative offset both return 400; confirmed the no-query-params default returns limit=50/offset=0 with all 5 rows.
case_management_api_hardening.scope_note: No new endpoints, no schema changes, no UI changes. Response shape addition (total/limit/offset alongside the existing cases array) is additive, not breaking.

legalcase_schema_alignment.status_note: Product Owner-approved architectural decision (Option 1 of 3 presented): additive LegalCase columns, Matter explicitly deferred to its own future milestone.
legalcase_schema_alignment.description: Added real, structural columns to the existing LegalCase table — status (TEXT, CHECK constrained to PENDING/HEARING/DISPOSED/APPEAL, NOT NULL DEFAULT 'PENDING'), court, judge, stage, hearing_date (TEXT, YYYY-MM-DD — deliberately not DATE, to avoid a UTC/timezone conversion footgun for a field nothing yet does date arithmetic on), and notes. All additive (ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS with a DEFAULT for the NOT NULL one), so existing rows were preserved with zero rewrite and the migration is safe to re-run. Verified idempotent by re-running db/schema.sql twice against the same database with no errors. RLS (tenant_isolation_policy, FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY) was not touched and continues to cover the whole table including the new columns. /api/cases and /api/cases/[id] now validate, persist, return, and (list endpoint) filter by these fields.
legalcase_schema_alignment.scope_decision: Matter is explicitly OUT of scope — no Matter table was created, no matter_id column was added to LegalCase. This was a genuine architectural fork presented to the Product Owner (schema-less state_metadata blob vs. additive real columns vs. a full Matter table+FK); Product Owner chose additive real columns with Matter deferred, matching how the /cases UI actually uses these fields (the list page filters by status, which needs to be a real indexable value, not JSON).

cases_ui_wired_to_real_api.description: apps/web/src/app/cases/page.tsx and apps/web/src/app/cases/[id]/page.tsx no longer use lib/db/litigation-db.ts (client-side localStorage/globalThis mock store) for Case data — they now fetch/create/update against the real /api/cases and /api/cases/[id] endpoints, with real loading and 401-unauthenticated states (a link to /login) instead of silently rendering empty. The 'Linked Parent Matter' selector was removed from the create-case form and the case-detail page's 'Bound Parent Matter' section was removed entirely, rather than faked against a table that doesn't exist. A required Jurisdiction Pack (country_code) selector was added to the create form — the one field the real schema requires that the mock form never had to model — reusing the same IN/US/UK options and visual style already used elsewhere in the app (e.g. the admin console). lib/db/litigation-db.ts itself is untouched and still used by /matters and /matters/[id] (out of scope, not modified) — only Case-related mock calls were removed from the two pages actually being migrated.
cases_ui_wired_to_real_api.verification: Verified with a real running server and a real headless-browser session (not just unit tests): unauthenticated /cases shows the auth-required state rather than crashing; a freshly-seeded tenant shows the real empty state; a case created through the real UI form appears in the real list with its real HEARING status rendered; filtering to PENDING correctly hides it; opening the case detail page shows the real title/court fields; saving notes through the UI persists via a real PATCH request and survives a full page reload (proving it's server-backed, not local component state); the parent-Matter section is confirmed absent. Zero console errors observed.
cases_ui_wired_to_real_api.scope_note: Only apps/web/src/app/cases/page.tsx and apps/web/src/app/cases/[id]/page.tsx were touched. apps/web/src/app/cases/new/page.tsx (an already-static placeholder with no mock-data dependency) and every /matters page are untouched.
cases_ui_wired_to_real_api.not_yet_true: No Matter persistence or case-to-matter linkage — recorded as its own separate future domain milestone, not silently dropped.

document_envelope_persistence.status: COMPLETE — metadata only, object storage still pending
document_envelope_persistence.status_note: POST /api/documents/upload was already tenant-authorized (server_enforced_tenant_authorization milestone) but explicitly documented as a mock with no real persistence — this milestone closes exactly that gap, at the metadata layer. Real object storage for the file bytes themselves is out of scope, per Product Owner priority ordering (item 4, a separate future milestone) — storage_structure honestly documents this as pending rather than pretending bytes are stored anywhere.
document_envelope_persistence.description: POST /api/documents/upload now persists a real DocumentEnvelope row (id, tenant_id, case_id, title, storage_structure, created_at) instead of returning a fake crypto.randomUUID(). Added GET /api/documents (paginated list, optional ?case_id= filter) and GET/DELETE /api/documents/[id], all tenant-scoped via the same requireSession()+DatabaseClient.execute(tenantId,...) pattern as /api/cases. Upload now accepts an optional x-case-id header to link a document to a real LegalCase.
document_envelope_persistence.security_finding_and_fix: Real end-to-end testing (not just unit tests) surfaced a genuine bug during this milestone: PostgreSQL foreign key constraint checks always bypass row-level security by design ('Referential integrity checks ... always bypass row security' — documented Postgres behavior) — so relying on the DocumentEnvelope.case_id -> LegalCase(id) FK alone silently accepted a case_id belonging to a DIFFERENT tenant (confirmed with a live cross-tenant request returning 202 before the fix). Fixed by adding an explicit, RLS-scoped SELECT ownership check on case_id before the insert is allowed to proceed — the FK remains for referential integrity, but is no longer relied on for tenant isolation. Verified fixed both in the test suite and against a real running server (a cross-tenant case_id now correctly returns 400).
document_envelope_persistence.verification: Verified against a real running server and real Postgres: uploaded a document, confirmed it round-trips through the real list/get endpoints, confirmed a different tenant's list/get correctly excludes/404s it, confirmed case_id linkage works for the caller's own case and is correctly rejected (400) for another tenant's case, confirmed case_id filtering on the list endpoint, confirmed DELETE removes it and a subsequent GET 404s.
document_envelope_persistence.scope_note: No object storage integration. No changes to /api/cases or the Cases UI. middleware.ts's self-authorized-route exemption was broadened from /api/documents/upload specifically to all of /api/documents, since the new list/detail routes authorize themselves the same way.
document_envelope_persistence.not_yet_true: storage_structure only records byte count and a 'pending' storage_provider marker — no file bytes are durably stored anywhere yet. That is the next approved milestone (object storage integration).

object_storage_integration.description: POST /api/documents/upload now durably stores the actual file bytes via lib/storage/object-storage.ts — a provider-agnostic adapter using the standard @aws-sdk/client-s3 client against whatever S3-compatible endpoint S3_ENDPOINT/S3_BUCKET/S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID/S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY point at (real AWS S3, Supabase Storage's S3-compatible endpoint, MinIO, Cloudflare R2, etc. — nothing here is vendor-specific). Object keys are tenant- and document-namespaced (lib/storage/document-key.ts buildObjectKey: '{tenantId}/{documentId}/{sanitizedFileName}'), with path-traversal-safe filename sanitization and a file-type allowlist (PDF/DOC/DOCX/TXT/JPG/JPEG/PNG) enforced before any bytes are accepted. Added GET /api/documents/[id]/download (tenant-scoped fetch of the real stored bytes with correct Content-Type/Content-Disposition). DELETE /api/documents/[id] now also removes the underlying object, not just the metadata row (best-effort — a storage-side failure doesn't resurrect an already-deleted record). Object storage is a hard requirement once configured: uploads return 503 OBJECT_STORAGE_NOT_CONFIGURED if S3_* is unset, matching the DATABASE_URL/REDIS_URL posture rather than silently no-op'ing; env-validation.ts requires all four S3_* vars in production.
object_storage_integration.infrastructure_investigation: MinIO itself could not be run in this development/CI environment: Docker Hub image pull failed (registry-side 403) and direct binary download from dl.min.io is explicitly blocked by the environment's egress policy (confirmed via the proxy's relay-failure log, not something to route around). Investigated and found a real alternative: s3rver, a pure-Node package implementing the genuine S3 HTTP API (not a mock/stub) — smoke-tested and verified working end-to-end against the standard AWS SDK before adopting it. scripts/test/start-s3rver.js starts it for local dev/test and CI (CI: a background process step, since s3rver has no first-party Docker image to use as a services: container) — same role MinIO would have played, satisfying 'MinIO or another S3-compatible local service' from the Product Owner's instruction.
object_storage_integration.verification: Verified against a real running server, real Postgres, and a real S3-compatible server (s3rver) — not mocked at any layer: uploaded real file bytes, downloaded them back and confirmed a byte-for-byte match with correct Content-Type/Content-Disposition, confirmed a different tenant's download attempt 404s without ever touching storage, confirmed a disallowed file extension (.exe) is rejected before any bytes are accepted, confirmed DELETE removes both the metadata row and the real object (a subsequent download 404s because the object is genuinely gone, not just because the DB row is gone).
object_storage_integration.scope_note: No document parsing, OCR, search indexing, or AI processing — explicitly out of scope for this milestone. No changes to /api/cases or the Cases UI. The PR #71 metadata layer is otherwise preserved; storage_structure's shape changed from a 'pending' placeholder to real {storage_provider, object_key, content_type, bytes_stored, etag} once this milestone landed.

document_search_and_indexing.description: Implemented hybrid document search & indexing per the Product Owner's explicit architecture decision: PostgreSQL + pgvector as the sole search substrate (no Elasticsearch/OpenSearch/Meilisearch/Typesense). DocumentChunkVector — previously dead schema with no tenant_id/RLS and no code path ever writing to it — was extended additively: tenant_id (+ its own tenant_isolation_policy, ENABLE+FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY), content (TEXT), content_tsv (a GENERATED ALWAYS AS to_tsvector(...) STORED column, GIN-indexed — the full-text half), and embedding (a real vector(1536) pgvector column, HNSW-indexed with vector_cosine_ops — the similarity half). The legacy vector_array column is kept, unused, rather than dropped (additive-only migration discipline). Indexing pipeline (lib/search/indexing.ts): RLS-scoped fetch of the DocumentEnvelope, fetches the real stored object via the existing object-storage adapter, extracts plain text (lib/search/text-extraction.ts — a pluggable seam: only text/plain is supported today, PDF/DOC/image content types return a defined 'UNSUPPORTED_CONTENT_TYPE' SKIPPED result rather than a fake success, so OCR/parsers can be added later without redesigning the pipeline), chunks it (lib/search/chunking.ts — fixed-size character chunking with overlap), generates one embedding per chunk through a provider-agnostic abstraction (lib/search/embedding-provider.ts), and persists chunks (re-indexing replaces prior chunks for that envelope outright). Exposed as POST /api/documents/[id]/index. Embedding provider abstraction: no AI vendor is hardcoded. A generic HTTP client (lib/search/embedding-provider.ts HttpEmbeddingProvider) speaks the widely-adopted OpenAI embeddings API shape (POST {EMBEDDING_API_BASE_URL}/embeddings) when EMBEDDING_API_BASE_URL + EMBEDDING_API_KEY are set — works against OpenAI itself or any self-hosted/gateway server exposing that same shape (vLLM, Ollama's OpenAI-compatible mode, LiteLLM, etc). When unconfigured, a deterministic local 'feature hashing' provider (a real, if crude, bag-of-words embedding technique — not random noise) is used instead, so the full pipeline and hybrid search are exercisable end-to-end in dev/CI without a live AI vendor key. Hybrid search: GET /api/search?q=&case_id=&limit=&offset= combines the vector-similarity ranking and full-text ranking via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, k=60 — the standard constant used across hybrid-search implementations), avoiding the need to normalize two differently-scaled scores against each other. Tenant-scoped throughout: DocumentChunkVector's own tenant_id + RLS policy protects both the indexing writes and the search reads — the envelope_id foreign key to DocumentEnvelope is never relied on alone for isolation, consistent with the FK-bypasses-RLS lesson from PR #71.
document_search_and_indexing.verification: Real Postgres with the pgvector 0.6.0 extension actually installed and CREATE EXTENSION'd (not assumed); schema migration re-run twice confirming full idempotency; 26 new automated tests (chunking, text-extraction, vector-format, embedding-provider including a cosine-similarity sanity check that related text scores higher than unrelated text, indexDocument integration tests against real Postgres + s3rver, and both new route handlers) plus the full existing 191-test suite passing with zero regressions. Full production-mode runtime verification against a real `next start` server with real minted session JWTs: uploaded a real document, indexed it, searched for a matching phrase and confirmed the correct chunk came back with a higher fused score than an unrelated query, confirmed a second tenant's index-attempt on the first tenant's document returns 404, confirmed a second tenant's search never returns the first tenant's chunks even when their own vector-similarity ranking has no textual match to fall back on, and confirmed an unauthenticated index attempt returns 401.
document_search_and_indexing.architecture_decision: Per explicit Product Owner instruction: PostgreSQL + pgvector only, reusing DocumentChunkVector, embeddings behind a swappable provider abstraction, hybrid (vector + full-text) search, tenant isolation enforced throughout, and an indexing pipeline designed so OCR/parsers/future AI enrichment can plug in later without redesign.
document_search_and_indexing.scope_note: Explicitly excluded per Product Owner instruction: LLM chat, RAG answer generation, OCR, AI summarization, UI redesign, external search engines. No changes to /dashboard/search or /search pages (existing stub UI pages untouched). packages/search-engine remains the pre-existing unused stub scaffold — not wired to anything and out of scope to touch.
document_search_and_indexing.known_gap_documented_not_blocking: The HNSW index (idx_documentchunkvector_embedding_hnsw) is built against whatever data exists at migration time — fine functionally at any volume, but a production deployment with a large pre-existing corpus indexed all at once may want a REINDEX after bulk-loading for optimal graph quality. Real semantic search quality depends on EMBEDDING_API_BASE_URL/EMBEDDING_API_KEY being set in production — without them the always-available local-hashing fallback keeps the feature functional but not semantically meaningful beyond shared-vocabulary overlap; this is a soft (non-blocking) requirement rather than a hard env-validation failure, since unlike S3/Redis the feature degrades gracefully rather than 503ing.

brand_color_uniformity_and_landing_fixes.description: Product Owner-reported visual bugs, fixed: (1) a literal blank 'white patch' on the landing page caused by the root layout adding a redundant 64px top padding on <main> on top of the navbar's own height (navbar is `sticky`/in-flow, not `fixed`, so it already reserved that space) — removed the redundant padding; (2) the hero's bookshelf background image was nearly invisible under an 85-95%-opaque dark overlay — reduced to 25-80% so it reads as an actual background while keeping hero text readable; (3) the hero's 'product preview' panel was a static AI-generated screenshot with a large blank canvas, a stray monitor-stand graphic cut off at the bottom, and off-brand blue/indigo 'LexCase AI' branding — replaced with a real JSX mockup rendered in the site's own gold/brown/cream palette (no external image dependency for this element). Internal (authenticated) pages — dashboard shell (apps/web/src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx), the TriPaneChamber AI workspace, the admin console (apps/web/src/app/admin/page.tsx + all 9 admin/components/*.tsx files), cases, matters, and login — previously used indigo/blue/gray/slate/neutral Tailwind color tokens throughout, visually disconnected from the landing page's gold/maroon/brown/cream palette. Every such token was remapped to the brand palette (warm neutrals for gray/slate/neutral shades, gold/brown for indigo/blue shades), and the login page's primary button was changed from plain black to the same gold/brown treatment used for CTAs elsewhere. Also fixed a latent bug: tailwind.config.ts's `bg-bg-base`/`bg-bg-surface`/`text-text-primary`/`text-brand` utilities referenced CSS custom properties (--color-bg-base, --color-brand-accent, etc.) that were never actually declared anywhere in the codebase — those classes were silently resolving to no color at all wherever used (found on the /audit and /search stub pages). Defined the properties for real in globals.css, and extended tailwind.config.ts with named brand.gold/brand.brown/brand.maroon/brand.ink tokens so future work has real semantic classes instead of ad hoc hex.
brand_color_uniformity_and_landing_fixes.verification: Full pre-existing 191-test suite, typecheck, and production build all pass with zero regressions (styling-only diff, no behavioral/API changes). Visually verified via a real `next start` production server plus headless Chromium screenshots, before and after, across: landing page (hero image visibility + white-patch gap), /login, /cases, /matters, /dashboard (TriPaneChamber), and /admin — confirmed the gap is gone, the bookshelf image is clearly visible, the hero preview panel shows real NextCaseHQ branding in brand colors, and every internal page renders in gold/brown/cream/black rather than blue/gray.
brand_color_uniformity_and_landing_fixes.scope_note: Visual/branding consistency pass only, applied to already-existing pages — no workflow changes, no new features, no page redesigns. Public marketing subpages (about, contact, docs, features, pricing, privacy, resources, solutions, terms) were not touched in this pass since the Product Owner's complaint was specifically about the landing-vs-internal-page mismatch; a few of those pages still use indigo/blue tokens and are visible next as tech debt if a further consistency pass is wanted.
brand_color_uniformity_and_landing_fixes.standing_uiux_directive: Product Owner has established a standing UI/UX directive: maintain the gold/maroon/brown/black/light-cream palette as the single design language across the whole platform going forward, applied incrementally as pages are touched during implementation (not a dedicated redesign effort) — navigation, sidebars, buttons, cards, tables, forms, badges, and typography should all read as one unified system rather than independently styled modules.

billing_and_messaging_foundations.status: COMPLETE — foundations only, no payment processor or outbound delivery yet
billing_and_messaging_foundations.description: Step 7 scoping investigation found billing and messaging were essentially unimplemented: TenantWallet/WalletTransactionRecord existed in schema with zero API/UI wiring, packages/messaging was a trivial unused console.log stub, and the dashboard's notification bell was hardcoded mock data. Split into what's implementable without a vendor decision (this milestone) vs. what needs one (flagged, not built). Real security gap found and fixed: WalletTransactionRecord had no tenant_id/RLS at all — only the wallet_id FK to TenantWallet, which (per the established FK-bypasses-RLS lesson) does not enforce tenant isolation on its own. Added tenant_id (+ ENABLE/FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY + tenant_isolation_policy), backfilled from the wallet_id join, additive migration verified idempotent. New GET /api/wallet (lazily creates a zero-balance wallet on first read via ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE...RETURNING, so every tenant has one without requiring a payment event) and GET /api/wallet/transactions (paginated, tenant-scoped ledger read). New Notification table (tenant_id + RLS, nullable user_id for tenant-wide notifications vs. one addressed to a specific user) with GET /api/notifications (personal + tenant-wide, ?unread_only= filter, unread_count) and PATCH /api/notifications/[id] (mark read; 404 rather than leak existence for another user's notification, a different tenant's notification, or an already-read one). Dashboard's notification bell (apps/web/src/app/dashboard/layout.tsx) rewired from a hardcoded three-item mock array to a real fetch, with a real unread-count-driven dot indicator and click-to-mark-read.
billing_and_messaging_foundations.verification: 18 new tests (wallet lazy-create + no-duplicate + cross-tenant isolation, wallet transactions RLS + pagination, notifications personal/tenant-wide/cross-user/cross-tenant scoping, mark-read including the 404-on-already-read and 404-on-wrong-user cases) plus the full pre-existing 191-test suite — 209 total, zero regressions. Real production-server verification: minted a session, hit GET /api/wallet (confirmed lazy zero-balance creation), GET /api/wallet/transactions, seeded a real Notification row directly in Postgres, confirmed it appears with unread_count:1, marked it read via PATCH, confirmed unread_count drops to 0, and confirmed the dashboard bell (real headless-browser screenshot) renders the real notification with the correct type badge, title, message, and relative timestamp in brand colors.
billing_and_messaging_foundations.scope_note: Deliberately does NOT include: real payment collection (accepting money, updating TenantWallet.balance from a real transaction) or real outbound message delivery (email/SMS) — both require a Product Owner decision on a vendor (payment processor; email/SMS provider) plus real API credentials, flagged separately rather than picked unilaterally. Nothing yet produces real Notification rows from application events (e.g. an actual upcoming-hearing reminder or a billing event) — the read/mark-read API is real and fully functional, but the 'producer' side is deferred until there's a real event to notify about.

ai_provider_abstraction_and_rag.status: COMPLETE — provider abstraction + RAG pipeline built and tested with mocked providers; real end-to-end verification pending production credentials
ai_provider_abstraction_and_rag.description: Product Owner architecture decision: provider-agnostic AI abstraction, OpenAI as the first supported provider, Anthropic as the second, keeping the existing pgvector/PostgreSQL RAG architecture from the search milestone. lib/ai/types.ts defines the vendor-agnostic LLMProvider interface (generateChatCompletion); lib/ai/providers/openai-provider.ts and anthropic-provider.ts are the two concrete implementations using the official openai and @anthropic-ai/sdk packages — nothing outside lib/ai/providers/ imports a vendor SDK directly. lib/ai/llm-provider.ts is the factory: AI_PROVIDER env var selects openai (default) or anthropic, each gated on its own API key env var, throwing a clear AIProviderNotConfiguredError otherwise (no silent no-op). lib/ai/retry.ts implements shared exponential-backoff retry logic, retrying only on 429/5xx/network errors (never on a 4xx like a bad API key, which fails identically every retry). lib/ai/rag.ts implements RAG answer generation: retrieves the top matching chunks via the existing hybrid search (extracted into lib/search/hybrid-search.ts so this doesn't need an HTTP round-trip to its own /api/search route), returns a defined NO_CONTEXT_FOUND result without ever calling the LLM if nothing relevant is indexed (avoids ungrounded hallucination and wastes no API call), otherwise assembles a system prompt instructing citation-only grounded answers plus numbered context excerpts, and returns the answer with structured source citations. Exposed as POST /api/ai/ask (tenant-scoped, origin-checked, 503 if unconfigured, 502 on upstream provider failure, 422 on no context found). The dashboard's AI Dialogue Stream (components/TriPaneChamber.tsx) — previously dead code that didn't even call its own local keyword-matching handler — is now wired to this real endpoint.
ai_provider_abstraction_and_rag.verification: 33 new tests: retry-logic edge cases, provider-factory selection (openai default/anthropic/unconfigured/invalid), OpenAI and Anthropic providers with the vendor SDKs mocked at the module level (success, 429-then-succeed, 429-exhausts-retries, non-retryable 4xx), RAG orchestration logic (grounds in retrieved chunks, returns NO_CONTEXT_FOUND without calling the LLM, passes case_id through for scoping), and the route handler (auth/origin/validation, and the NOT_CONFIGURED/REQUEST_FAILED/NO_CONTEXT/success status-code mapping). Full pre-existing suite unaffected (242 total, zero regressions) — includes a behavior-preserving refactor of GET /api/search onto the newly-extracted hybridSearch() function, re-verified against its existing tests. pnpm typecheck and pnpm build both pass. Real production-server verification up to the credential boundary: uploaded and indexed a real document, confirmed POST /api/ai/ask correctly returns 422 NO_CONTEXT_FOUND with no indexed documents, 503 AI_PROVIDER_NOT_CONFIGURED once real context exists but no vendor key is set, and correct 401/400 for missing auth/invalid body — this is the exact boundary the Product Owner specified: 'stop only when real provider credentials are required for end-to-end verification.'
ai_provider_abstraction_and_rag.scope_note: No hardcoded API keys or account identifiers anywhere — AI_PROVIDER/OPENAI_API_KEY/OPENAI_MODEL/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY/ANTHROPIC_MODEL are all read from env only. Not added to env-validation.ts's hard production-blocking checks (unlike S3/Redis) — this is a soft, non-blocking requirement matching the embedding-provider treatment: the feature cleanly 503s without a key rather than breaking any existing capability, so production can deploy without AI configured if that's the Product Owner's call. Explicitly excludes: multi-turn conversation memory beyond what's already client-side in TriPaneChamber, streaming responses, and any third AI provider beyond the two named.

uiux_platform_refinement_directive.status: COMPLETE — first incremental series delivered per the standing directive
uiux_platform_refinement_directive.description: Product Owner issued a comprehensive platform-wide UI/UX refinement directive (visual polish only — explicitly not a feature/workflow/architecture change): every landing page should communicate value within the first viewport on a 13-15in laptop (complete headline, description, CTA(s), search bar, and a substantially-visible dashboard/application preview), a unified gold/maroon/brown/black/light-cream design language across auth + internal pages, no visually empty/unfinished-looking pages, subtle premium background treatments (never competing with readability), avoidance of gaudy/flashy effects (oversaturated color, excessive gradients/shadows, neon, clutter), and tasteful empty states — delivered incrementally with responsiveness/visual verification before each merge, per the Product Owner's explicit instruction. Four PRs landed this series: (1) PR #77 (hero above-the-fold fix, pre-dates this directive but addressed the identical white-patch/hero-visibility complaint that reappeared in the directive's own section 1 — confirmed still compliant on re-check, no further changes needed); (2) PR #79 — a shared BrandBackground component (soft gold gradient wash + a faint scales-of-justice line-art watermark) applied to previously flat white/cream pages: login, the dashboard shell, and the /cases and /matters list pages, most visible in empty states and margins without ever competing with opaque content. Found and fixed a genuine CSS stacking-context bug along the way: a negative z-index escaped past the intended container (since `position: relative` alone doesn't establish a new stacking context) and rendered invisibly behind the document root's own background — fixed with `isolate` on each host container; (3) PR #80 — toned down three overly heavy hero shadow values (shadow-2xl at 40-50% black opacity) to softer, more restrained equivalents, per the directive's explicit 'avoid heavy shadows' guidance. Modal/drawer shadow-2xl usage elsewhere was deliberately left alone — Tailwind's unmodified default is a conventional, tasteful choice for true overlay UI, not the case being targeted; (4) PR #81 — replaced bare OS emoji in empty states (including an off-brand blue-rendering scales-of-justice glyph) with a shared EmptyState component: a consistent gold-tinted circular icon badge, applied to the cases list, matters list, and the notification drawer, each with a direct create-action wired to the existing create-form toggle.
uiux_platform_refinement_directive.verification: Each PR independently verified via the full pre-existing test suite (191 through 242 tests as the suite grew across this session, zero regressions at each step — all four PRs are pure CSS/JSX styling diffs with no behavioral changes), pnpm typecheck, pnpm build, and real production-server screenshots via headless Chromium at multiple viewport sizes (1440x816, 1366x768, 1280x720 laptop sizes; 390x844 mobile; 768x1024/1024x768 tablet) confirming the visual result at each step before merging, exactly matching the Product Owner's 'validate responsiveness and visual consistency before each merge' instruction.
uiux_platform_refinement_directive.scope_note: Strictly visual/usability — no workflow, navigation, business logic, or architecture changes in any of the four PRs, consistent with the directive's explicit 'Preserve Existing Work' section. Public marketing subpages beyond the landing hero (about, contact, docs, features, pricing, privacy, resources, solutions, terms) and the admin console were not touched in this series — the admin console specifically was judged already content-dense enough that a decorative background would add noise rather than address an actual 'empty/unfinished' gap; further passes there remain a candidate for a future incremental round if desired.
uiux_platform_refinement_directive.standing_directive_carryover: The Product Owner's directive is explicitly a standing constraint on all future UI work, not a one-time pass — subsequent milestones (Payments, Messaging, further AI features) should continue to apply this same premium/restrained/consistent visual language as their pages are touched, per the Product Owner's own framing: 'Deliver the refinements incrementally... as pages are touched during implementation.'

payments_stripe_abstraction.status: COMPLETE — up to the real-credentials boundary
payments_stripe_abstraction.description: Product Owner architecture decision: Stripe as the first payment provider, keeping the billing layer provider-agnostic. lib/billing/types.ts defines the vendor-agnostic PaymentProvider interface (createCheckoutSession, parseWebhookEvent); lib/billing/providers/stripe-provider.ts is the concrete implementation using the official stripe npm package — nothing outside lib/billing/providers/ imports the Stripe SDK directly. lib/billing/payment-provider.ts is the factory: PAYMENT_PROVIDER selects the vendor (only 'stripe' supported today, ready for a second without touching any call site), gated on STRIPE_SECRET_KEY + STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET both being set, throwing a clear PaymentProviderNotConfiguredError otherwise. Retry logic is Stripe's own SDK built-in exponential backoff with idempotency keys (maxNetworkRetries: 2) rather than a redundant second retry layer. POST /api/billing/checkout creates a real Stripe Checkout Session for a tenant wallet top-up (amount in the smallest currency unit, e.g. paise), carrying the tenant id through as Checkout Session metadata since the webhook call has no session cookie to read it from. POST /api/billing/webhook verifies Stripe's own webhook signature scheme (Stripe-Signature header, HMAC-SHA256, via the SDK's constructEvent — never trusts an unverified payload), and on a checkout.session.completed event, credits the tenant's TenantWallet.balance and records a WalletTransactionRecord, idempotently (a repeated delivery of the same event, identified by the Stripe checkout session id stored in the transaction's metadata, is a no-op rather than a double-credit).
payments_stripe_abstraction.verification: 24 new tests: Stripe provider with the vendor SDK mocked at the module level (checkout session creation success/failure, webhook signature verification success/failure, event-type filtering, metadata extraction), provider-factory selection, and route-level tests (auth/origin/validation, and the NOT_CONFIGURED/REQUEST_FAILED/IGNORED/CREDITED/ALREADY_PROCESSED status mapping) — the webhook route's DB-crediting logic is tested against a real Postgres instance, not mocked. Full pre-existing suite unaffected — 266 total, zero regressions. pnpm typecheck and pnpm build both pass. Real production-server verification exercising the actual (non-mocked) Stripe SDK: POST /api/billing/checkout with a real (fake-value) secret key correctly attempts a real network call to Stripe's API and surfaces the resulting failure as a clean 502 rather than crashing — this is the exact boundary requiring real credentials. POST /api/billing/webhook's signature verification is pure local HMAC (no network call), so it was verified fully for real: constructed a genuinely valid Stripe-style signature (t=<timestamp>,v1=<hmac>) by hand using the same algorithm Stripe uses, confirmed an invalid signature is rejected with 401, confirmed a valid signature with a real checkout.session.completed-shaped payload correctly credits the wallet (verified the real Postgres row: 123400 paise -> balance 1234.00), and confirmed replaying the identical event is idempotent (second delivery returns ALREADY_PROCESSED, no second transaction row).
payments_stripe_abstraction.scope_note: No API keys or account identifiers hardcoded anywhere — PAYMENT_PROVIDER/STRIPE_SECRET_KEY/STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET/NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL are all read from env only. Not added to env-validation.ts's hard production-blocking checks (unlike S3/Redis) — matches the AI provider's soft-requirement treatment: the checkout/webhook routes cleanly 503 without configuration rather than breaking any existing capability. Explicitly excludes: subscription/recurring billing (this milestone is a one-time wallet top-up flow only), refunds, and any UI page for triggering a checkout (no dashboard 'Add Funds' button was added — the API is real and ready, but wiring a UI entry point was judged out of scope for a backend-focused provider-abstraction milestone).

uiux_directive_validation_pass.status: COMPLETE — 2 confirmed bugs fixed; 2 larger pre-existing gaps flagged for Product Owner decision, not silently expanded or silently skipped
uiux_directive_validation_pass.description: Product Owner requested a full validation-and-sign-off pass against the running application for the earlier UI/UX platform refinement directive (uiux_platform_refinement_directive above), rather than assuming the prior incremental PRs fully satisfied it. Real headless-Chromium screenshots were captured for every public page (landing, about, features, pricing, solutions, resources, docs, contact, privacy, terms) and /login at 4 breakpoints (1920x1080 desktop, 1366x768 13in laptop, 768x1024 tablet, 390x844 mobile), plus full-page captures to check for below-the-fold blank/unfinished areas. Two concrete, unambiguous bugs were found and fixed: (1) /docs, /privacy, /resources, and /terms each rendered the Navbar component TWICE — once from the global root layout's NavbarWrapper (which renders Navbar on every route except /login, /organization, and the dashboard/admin/system route groups) and a second, redundant time from each page's own component tree. Fixed by removing the redundant local <Navbar /> import/render from all four pages, relying solely on the global wrapper, matching how every other public page (about/features/pricing/solutions/contact) already worked. (2) The /login page's 'HQ' wordmark rendered in grey (text-[#111111]/60) while the shared Navbar's identical 'HQ' wordmark renders in brand gold (#E4C77E) — an inconsistent, off-palette rendering of the same brand mark on two different pages. Fixed by recoloring /login's 'HQ' to #8A6D2F (a darker gold already used elsewhere in this app's light-background contexts, e.g. the hero's 'Access your chamber' button and the BrandBackground watermark stroke, chosen over the Navbar's lighter #E4C77E specifically for adequate contrast against /login's light cream background).
uiux_directive_validation_pass.verification: Full pre-existing 266-test suite passing, zero regressions (both fixes are pure JSX/class-name diffs with no behavioral changes). pnpm typecheck and pnpm build both pass. Real production-server before/after screenshots via headless Chromium confirm both fixes visually: the duplicate navbar is gone on all four affected pages, and the /login wordmark now matches the Navbar's established gold treatment.

flagged_not_fixed.description: The five marketing subpages /about, /features, /pricing, /solutions, and /contact still use their original indigo-600/neutral-100..600/blue-400 Tailwind color classes (never migrated to the gold/maroon/brown/black/light-cream palette the way the landing hero, dashboard, cases, matters, and login pages were in earlier milestones), raw colorful OS emoji as icon badges (a book, lightning bolt, credit card, envelope, lock, and scales-of-justice glyph — the exact category of thing PR #81's EmptyState component was built to eliminate elsewhere), a large blank area between the navbar and each page's icon badge/heading at desktop and laptop widths, and bespoke ad hoc <footer> markup instead of the shared Footer component used by every other public page. This is a real, confirmed deviation from the directive's 'unified color system' and 'avoid gaudy appearance' sections — but it is also a substantial, ~5-page, systemic recolor-and-componentize effort (each page independently touches color classes, icon treatment, and header/footer markup), explicitly pre-flagged as an open, not-yet-assigned gap in this same milestone's own prior scope_note ('Public marketing subpages beyond the landing hero... were not touched in this series... remain candidates for a future incremental round if desired'). Not fixed in this pass — flagged for the Product Owner to confirm before undertaking, given its size relative to the two mechanical bugs above.
flagged_not_fixed.resolution: APPROVED and RESOLVED — see marketing_pages_brand_refinement below. Product Owner approved the full recolor/componentize effort; completed in a follow-up PR.

flagged_not_fixed.description: The site's primary 'dark' surface color, used site-wide (Navbar, HeroSection, the Security landing section, CtaBand, SiteFooter, and the Logo icon itself) is #0E241B — a dark forest green (R=14, G=36, B=27; the green channel is dominant). It reads as a plausible near-black at a glance in every screenshot captured, and long pre-dates this validation pass (set during an earlier brand-color-uniformity milestone), but the directive's 'Unified color system' section is explicit that the approved palette is 'strictly Gold/Maroon/Brown/Black/Light Cream' — a literal hex-level check finds this color outside that list. Given how pervasively it's used as the site's primary dark anchor color (including the logo mark itself), changing it is a highly visible, high-blast-radius brand decision, not a mechanical bug fix — flagged for the Product Owner to confirm whether the current dark-green-reading-as-black is acceptable as-is, or whether it should be shifted toward a literal near-neutral black/deep-brown.
flagged_not_fixed.resolution: RESOLVED — Product Owner explicitly approved #0E241B as a permanent, named part of the brand palette ('Deep Forest Green'), not a deviation. The approved palette is now: Gold, Deep Forest Green (#0E241B), Maroon (used selectively, not forced for its own sake), Brown, Light Cream, and White for content surfaces. No code change required — #0E241B usage across Navbar/Hero/Security/CtaBand/Footer/Logo is retained as-is.

marketing_pages_brand_refinement.description: Product Owner-approved follow-up to uiux_directive_validation_pass's flagged gap: full visual refinement of the five marketing subpages (/about, /features, /pricing, /solutions, /contact) to bring them into the same design system already established for the landing page and auth pages — explicitly not a redesign, with information architecture, copy, navigation, and functionality all preserved. Replaced every indigo-600/neutral-100..600 Tailwind class with the approved palette (#111111 text, #FDFBF7/#FBF6EA cream surfaces, #8A6D2F/#C6A253 gold accents). Replaced all raw colorful OS emoji (book, lightning bolt, lock, scales, pen, credit card, globe, building, document, person, envelope) with a consistent set of minimal line-art SVG icons (viewBox 40x40, #8A6D2F stroke, matching the exact visual language already established by the cases-list EmptyState scales-of-justice icon), each in a circular gold-tinted badge matching EmptyState's own treatment. Eliminated the vertical-centering layout pattern (flex-1 flex flex-col justify-center items-center plus a redundant pt-16) that was the actual root cause of the large blank areas above each heading — replaced with the same max-w-7xl mx-auto px-6 py-20 lg:py-32 pattern already used correctly by the four already-compliant content pages (docs/privacy/resources/terms), which does not fight the sticky Navbar's own in-flow height the way the old pt-16 did. Replaced every page's bespoke ad hoc <footer> with the shared Footer component (also fixing a latent bug: the ad hoc footers' Privacy/Terms/Support links all pointed at /login instead of the real pages). Feature/solution grids and the pricing card now use the shared Card and Badge components, matching the resources page's established idiom exactly.
marketing_pages_brand_refinement.verification: Full pre-existing 266-test suite passing, zero regressions (styling/markup-only diffs, no behavioral changes — confirmed a same-suite failure during this work was pre-existing Redis rate-limit state bleeding across parallel Jest workers, not a regression, by reproducing it in isolation then re-running clean with --runInBand after a Redis flush). pnpm typecheck and pnpm build both pass. grep-verified zero remaining indigo-/neutral-/blue-/slate-/gray- Tailwind classes and zero raw emoji across all public/auth pages. Real production-server screenshots via headless Chromium across all 4 breakpoints (1920x1080 desktop, 1366x768 13in laptop, 768x1024 tablet, 390x844 mobile) for all five pages confirm: brand-consistent icons and palette, no large blank areas, correct shared Footer with working links, and correct responsive behavior down to mobile.
marketing_pages_brand_refinement.scope_note: Strictly visual/markup — no workflow, navigation, copy, or business-logic changes; every heading, paragraph, link destination (other than the footer link fix, which was a bug, not a feature), and CTA target is unchanged. The Badge component's accent gold (#C5A059) is a very slightly different hex from the #C6A253/#8A6D2F used elsewhere in this pass — a pre-existing, minor inconsistency in the shared component itself, not introduced here and left as-is per Governance Alignment (no unrelated refactoring).

governance_alignment_check.security_and_tenant_isolation_impact: None — no server-side code, database access, or authentication logic touched. All five pages are static/public marketing content with no tenant context.
governance_alignment_check.architectural_compatibility: Strengthens existing patterns rather than bypassing them: reuses the already-established Footer/Badge/Card shared components and the exact icon-badge visual language from EmptyState.tsx instead of inventing a new one, and fixes a layout anti-pattern (redundant pt-16 fighting the sticky Navbar's own in-flow height) that the root layout.tsx already carries an explicit code comment warning against.
governance_alignment_check.scalability_and_maintainability_impact: Positive — five previously divergent, hand-rolled pages now follow the same reusable component idiom as the rest of the public site, reducing the number of distinct patterns a future change has to account for.
governance_alignment_check.technical_debt: Introduced: none (no new abstractions beyond what already existed). Eliminated: the entire indigo/neutral legacy Tailwind theme is now fully gone from every public and auth page (grep-verified), and the four broken footer links (Privacy/Terms/Support pointing at /login) are fixed as a side effect of adopting the shared Footer.
governance_alignment_check.assumptions_or_divergence_requiring_appr: None remaining — both items flagged in uiux_directive_validation_pass were explicitly resolved by direct Product Owner decision before this milestone began (marketing-page recolor approved; #0E241B retained as an approved palette color).

matter_workspace_foundation.description: Milestone 1 of the Product Owner-approved Advocate Workspace roadmap — the foundational Matter entity. Product Owner-confirmed architecture: a Matter is the parent client engagement and digital case room; the existing LegalCase becomes a child Proceeding linked via a new nullable matter_id FK (a single column, not a join table, so a Proceeding has at most zero-or-one parent Matter by construction). A Matter may exist with zero Proceedings — engagement_type is CHECK-constrained to LITIGATION/PRE_LITIGATION/ADVISORY/CONTRACTUAL/TRANSACTIONAL/ARBITRATION/MEDIATION/COMPLIANCE/INVESTIGATION/OTHER, so pre-litigation, advisory, and transactional work are first-class, not litigation-shaped by default. New tables (all tenant_id + ENABLE/FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY + tenant_isolation_policy, same pattern as every existing table): Client (minimal — name/email/phone/notes, no CRM features), Matter (title/matter_number/engagement_type/practice_area/status/client_id/opposing_party/court/bench/judge/description/opened_at/closed_at), MatterParticipant (team assignment record only — role enum LEAD/ASSOCIATE/CLERK/VIEWER stored for a future permissions milestone to enforce against, no access-control logic added yet), MatterEvent (chronology — source_type restricted to MANUAL today, HEARING/ORDER/DOCUMENT reserved for future milestones that generate events automatically). LegalCase.matter_id is additive and nullable (ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS) — every pre-existing LegalCase row and query keeps working unchanged. New APIs mirroring the existing /api/cases conventions exactly (requireSession + isTrustedOrigin + zod validation + DatabaseClient.execute(tenantId,...)): GET/POST /api/matters, GET/PATCH/DELETE /api/matters/[id], GET/POST /api/matters/[id]/events, GET/POST /api/matters/[id]/participants, GET/POST /api/clients, GET/PATCH/DELETE /api/clients/[id]. /api/cases and /api/cases/[id] extended with an optional matter_id field/filter. Every route accepting a client_id or matter_id FK re-verifies ownership via an RLS-scoped SELECT before trusting it, per the established FK-bypasses-RLS lesson (a foreign key constraint check always bypasses RLS). The real /matters list and /matters/[id] detail pages now fetch this real API — lib/db/litigation-db.ts (the client-side mock store both pages previously used, with its own fictional Case shape unrelated to the real LegalCase table) was deleted outright, its two importers being the only two files that used it. The Matter detail page ('digital case room') shows real Proceedings (LegalCase rows filtered by matter_id), real Chronology, and a real Team panel, plus honest 'Not yet available' placeholders for Documents/Evidence/Tasks/Drafts & Research — no fabricated data for milestones that haven't landed. The two previously fake, permanently-disabled 'Lock Matter Ledger'/'Compliance Check (BNSS)' sidebar buttons were removed rather than carried forward. lib/ai/matter-context.ts (getMatterContext) assembles a Matter's title/status/client/linked-Proceedings/Chronology into a single text summary, seeding the future Matter Memory feature for RAG — wiring it into /api/ai/ask's prompt is out of this milestone's scope (no chat-panel changes were made) but the context-assembly function itself is real, tested, and ready.
matter_workspace_foundation.verification: 48 new automated tests: cross-tenant RLS isolation for Client/Matter/MatterParticipant/MatterEvent and matter_id-linked LegalCase rows (lib/db/__tests__/matter-rls.test.ts), full route suites for /api/matters, /api/matters/[id], /api/matters/[id]/events, /api/matters/[id]/participants, and /api/clients, plus a dedicated backward-compatibility regression suite proving a LegalCase created with no matter_id persists and lists correctly with matter_id null, and that /api/cases?matter_id= filtering and PATCH-to-attach-a-matter-after-creation both work. Full pre-existing 266-test suite plus the 48 new tests all pass — 314 total, zero regressions. pnpm typecheck (all 15 workspace projects) and the root TypeScript project-graph validation both pass. Real runtime verification against a real running server (not `next start` on a stale build — an old production server from a prior session was found still listening on the target port and was killed first) and real Postgres: created a real Client and an ADVISORY-engagement Matter via the UI-equivalent API calls, linked a Proceeding under it, added a Chronology entry, confirmed the Matter detail page renders all three plus the honest empty Team/Documents/Evidence/Tasks/Drafts panels (headless-Chromium screenshots), confirmed a cross-tenant Matter ID request returns 404 (not a permission leak), and confirmed three genuinely pre-Milestone-1 LegalCase rows (matter_id NULL, seeded before this migration ever ran) still list and render correctly on /cases unchanged.
matter_workspace_foundation.scope_note: Deliberately narrow per the 10 Product Owner-stated conditions: no CRM, no collaboration/permissions enforcement, no billing, no evidence registry, no tasks, no OCR, no drafting features — MatterParticipant's role column is stored but not yet enforced by anything. LegalCase itself was not renamed or restructured beyond the one additive matter_id column. No migration rollback script exists (or exists anywhere else in this codebase) — db/schema.sql is forward-only, idempotent, additive SQL by established convention; 'rollback' for this milestone means dropping the newly added tables/column, not a scripted down-migration.
matter_workspace_foundation.not_yet_true: getMatterContext() is not yet wired into /api/ai/ask or the TriPaneChamber AI chat panel — the context-assembly function is real and tested, but actually feeding it into a live AI conversation is deferred to a future milestone per the blueprint's own scoping. MatterParticipant's role is a stored label only, with zero access-control enforcement. Related-Matters linking (Matter-to-Matter) was explicitly deferred by Product Owner instruction and not implemented.
out_of_scope_next: commercialization_and_abuse_prevention (trials/credits/subscriptions/usage-metering — explicitly a future billing milestone, not started)

governance_alignment_check.security_and_tenant_isolation_impact: All four new tables carry tenant_id + ENABLE/FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY + tenant_isolation_policy, identical to every existing table. Every new route re-verifies a client-supplied client_id/matter_id/user_id FK via an RLS-scoped SELECT before trusting it, since a foreign key constraint check always bypasses RLS by Postgres design — the same lesson learned and documented during the Document Envelope milestone (DB-12), applied proactively here rather than discovered as a live bug. Verified via 48 new RLS/route tests plus real cross-tenant 404 checks against a running server.
governance_alignment_check.architectural_compatibility: Matter is added as a new parent alongside LegalCase, not a replacement — LegalCase is untouched except for one additive, nullable FK column, satisfying the explicit 'do not destabilize the existing LegalCase model' condition. New API routes mirror /api/cases's established requireSession/isTrustedOrigin/zod/DatabaseClient.execute pattern exactly, so this is a horizontal extension of an existing, proven pattern rather than a new architecture.
governance_alignment_check.scalability_and_maintainability_impact: Positive — establishes the reusable 'matter_id FK + tenant_id + its own RLS policy' pattern that every future Matter sub-feature (Documents, Evidence, Tasks, Hearings, Drafts, Billing) can follow without re-deriving it, and removes a dead mock data layer (lib/db/litigation-db.ts) entirely rather than leaving it to rot alongside the real implementation.
governance_alignment_check.technical_debt: Introduced: none beyond what the milestone requires (MatterParticipant.role is stored ahead of its own enforcement milestone, by explicit Product Owner design, not an oversight). Eliminated: lib/db/litigation-db.ts (a client-side mock store with a fictional Case shape unrelated to the real LegalCase table) is fully removed, and the two previously-fake, permanently-disabled 'Litigation Audit Actions' buttons on the Matter detail page are gone rather than carried forward as dead UI.
governance_alignment_check.assumptions_or_divergence_requiring_appr: None — implemented exactly to the Product Owner's 10 stated conditions and the previously approved blueprint. The mid-milestone 'Commercialization & Abuse Prevention' vision (trials/credits/subscriptions/usage metering) was explicitly not implemented here, consistent with condition 8 ('do not prematurely implement... billing'), and is recorded above as future-milestone input, not scope creep.

matter_client_delete_fk_safety_hardening.description: Isolated post-Milestone-1 hardening patch, raised by a post-merge architectural verification of PR #87 before Milestone 2 began. LegalCase.matter_id and Matter.client_id both carry no ON DELETE action (Postgres default RESTRICT), so DELETE /api/matters/[id] and DELETE /api/clients/[id] previously surfaced a raw foreign-key-violation error as a generic 500 whenever the target still had linked records. Fixed by checking every dependent record type up front (Proceedings/MatterParticipant/MatterEvent for a Matter; Matter for a Client) via RLS-scoped COUNT queries before attempting the delete, returning a deterministic 409 with a stable machine-readable code (MATTER_HAS_LINKED_RECORDS / CLIENT_HAS_LINKED_MATTERS) and a concise counts payload (e.g. {proceedings, participants, events}) when anything is linked, plus a defense-in-depth catch around the actual DELETE that translates any foreign-key-violation error message into the same 409 rather than letting it fall through to the generic 500 handler (covers the narrow race between the check and the delete). Deliberately does not rely on cascading deletion: MatterParticipant and MatterEvent remain ON DELETE CASCADE at the schema level (unchanged, not touched by this patch), but the API enforces a stricter no-cascade policy on top of that by blocking the delete outright if either has any rows, rather than allowing the schema's cascade to silently remove them.
matter_client_delete_fk_safety_hardening.scope_note: Strictly the two DELETE handlers. No schema changes (the underlying ON DELETE clauses are untouched — this is an application-layer policy, not a constraint change). No delete/archive UI was added — both endpoints remain unreachable from the current UI, exactly as before. No archive functionality was introduced. Tenant isolation preserved throughout: every dependent-count query and the existence check itself run through the same session.tenantId-scoped DatabaseClient.execute(...) as every other route, so a cross-tenant id still resolves to 404 (not 409, and not a leak of whether the record or its dependents exist in another tenant) exactly as it did before this patch.
matter_client_delete_fk_safety_hardening.verification: 9 new tests (3 added to the existing apps/web/src/app/api/matters/__tests__/id-route.test.ts, 6 in a new apps/web/src/app/api/clients/__tests__/id-route.test.ts, which had no DELETE/GET/PATCH coverage at all before this patch): a Matter with zero dependents deletes cleanly; a Matter with a linked Proceeding returns 409/MATTER_HAS_LINKED_RECORDS and is confirmed to still exist afterward (the delete was refused, not partially applied); a Matter with only a MatterParticipant (zero Proceedings) and a separate Matter with only a MatterEvent (zero Proceedings) each independently return 409 — proving the check does not rely solely on catching LegalCase's FK-restrict error, since MatterParticipant/MatterEvent would otherwise cascade away silently; a cross-tenant Matter delete still returns 404; a Client with a linked Matter returns 409/CLIENT_HAS_LINKED_MATTERS and is confirmed to still exist; deleting the linked Matter first and retrying succeeds; a cross-tenant Client delete still returns 404. Full suite: 323 tests total (314 from PR #87 plus these 9), zero regressions — one pre-existing, already-documented flaky Redis rate-limit test (docs/PENDING_INTEGRATION_REGISTER.md's 'Observed: Flaky Redis-Backed Rate-Limit Tests' entry) failed once under parallel workers and passed cleanly on re-run in isolation with a fresh Redis state, confirmed unrelated to this patch (it touches POST /api/auth/session, untouched by this change). pnpm typecheck (all 15 workspace TypeScript projects) passes.
matter_client_delete_fk_safety_hardening.architectural_note_for_milestone_2: DocumentEnvelope currently links only to LegalCase.case_id, not to Matter. Before implementing Matter-level documents, evaluate and add a nullable DocumentEnvelope.matter_id attachment path (following the same matter_id + tenant_id + own RLS-policy pattern established throughout the Matter Workspace Foundation) so advisory and pre-litigation Matters — which may legitimately have zero Proceedings — can own documents without being forced to create a stub Proceeding first. This was raised during the post-merge architectural verification of PR #87 and is recorded here as input for Milestone 2's own schema design, not implemented in this patch.

governance_alignment_check.security_and_tenant_isolation_impact: Neutral-to-positive: no new RLS surface, no new tables. Every new query (the existence check and the dependent-count checks) runs through the existing session.tenantId-scoped DatabaseClient.execute(...) pattern, so cross-tenant behavior (404, not 409, not a leak) is unchanged from before this patch and was explicitly re-verified by test.
governance_alignment_check.architectural_compatibility: Additive, isolated change to two existing route handlers — no schema change, no new API surface, no change to any other route. Reuses the exact error-shape convention (error/code/message) already established by the participants route's own 409 handling for duplicate assignments, rather than inventing a new response shape.
governance_alignment_check.scalability_and_maintainability_impact: Positive — the pattern (check dependents up front, translate any FK violation as a fallback, never let a constraint error reach the client as a raw 500) is now demonstrated for two resources and is directly reusable by any future DELETE endpoint added to a table with restrict-by-default foreign keys.
governance_alignment_check.technical_debt: Introduced: none. Eliminated: the undocumented DELETE-safety gap flagged in the post-merge architectural verification of PR #87 is fully closed and now covered by tests.
governance_alignment_check.assumptions_or_divergence_requiring_appr: None — implemented exactly to the Product Owner's stated requirements (no cascading deletion, no schema change, no UI, no archive functionality, 409 with a stable code, tenant isolation preserved).
